title: Azure DevOps proxy for Git
excerpt: Does Azure DevOps 2019 Proxy server (formerly TFS proxy server) support Git repositories? The documentation only mentions TFVC repositories, and it works great, but we are not sure if Git repositories can be configured t…

posts.author: issac (issac louis)
posts.cookedHtml:
Does Azure DevOps 2019 Proxy server (formerly TFS proxy server) support Git repositories? The documentation only mentions TFVC repositories, and it works great, but we are not sure if Git repositories can be configured to use Azure DevOps (TFS) proxy (not to confuse with regular Web proxy). Appreciate any help!

Know someone who can answer?

posts.author: LillyBates (Lilly)
posts.cookedHtml: From what I’ve seen, Azure DevOps Proxy Server mainly mentions TFVC repositories, but Git repositories should work with it too.

posts.author: LillyBates (Lilly)
posts.cookedHtml: Well, I totally understand your concern – when it comes to managing repositories, you want to make sure everything runs smoothly, whether it’s TFVC or Git. While Azure DevOps Proxy Server documentation seems to focus more on TFVC repositories, Git repositories should be compatible, too.But you can always try another proxy server.If you’re still feeling uncertain, why not give it a shot with a small test repository? Sometimes hands-on experimentation is the best way to confirm compatibility and performance.

posts.author: Okudama (Oswaldo)
posts.cookedHtml: Well, I totally understand your concern – when it comes to managing repositories, you want to make sure everything runs smoothly, whether it’s TFVC or Git. While Azure DevOps Proxy Server documentation seems to focus more on TFVC repositories, Git repositories should be compatible, too.
